title: The 1960s in America: Crash Course US History #40
summary: John Green covers the major movements and events of the 1960s in America: the Civil Rights Movement (sit-ins, Freedom Rides, March on Washington, Civil Rights Act, Voting Rights Act), JFK's presidency and assassination, LBJ's Great Society programs (Medicare, Medicaid, War on Poverty), the Vietnam War and anti-war movement, the counterculture, the women's movement, Latino and Native American activism, the Stonewall uprising, and the Warren Court's expansion of civil liberties. The episode emphasizes that the 1960s changes were part of a long ongoing process rather than a sudden revolution.

fact_check_notes.claim: Letter from Birmingham Jail was written while MLK was jailed in Birmingham
March on Washington was the largest public demonstration in American history up to that time
JFK was initially cool to civil rights
fact_check_notes.assessment: accurate - JFK prioritized Cold War issues early in his presidency

fact_check_notes.claim: Gulf of Tonkin Resolution 1964 gave LBJ authority to escalate Vietnam
By 1968 over 500,000 American troops were in Vietnam
Stonewall Inn raid was in June 1969
Equal Pay Act was passed in 1963
Loving v. Virginia struck down laws banning interracial marriage
Griswold v. Connecticut and Roe v. Wade expanded privacy rights
fact_check_notes.assessment: partially_accurate - Griswold was 1965 (1960s) but Roe v. Wade was 1973 (not 1960s), though Green mentions both as part of the Warren Court era expansion

fact_check_notes.claim: Miranda v. Arizona required police to inform suspects of their rights
Betty Friedan's The Feminine Mystique published in 1963
